Based on the [Dialog History] provided, please answer the given [Question].

2. This question is being asked in the context of May 6, 2012.

[Question] In an intellectually charged debate held on September 29, 2009, upon which cricket species did Howard stake his prized comic books, contesting Sheldon's presumably superior entomological expertise?
[Answer]
raw_source_record.dialog_bm25_dialog:
[{"role": "user", "content": "Context:
Coversation [September 29, 2009, Session #316]:
Speaker Pennysays : Oh, hey, Sheldon. What you got there? A new comic book?
Speaker Sheldonsays : Old comic book. I just retrieved it from my safe deposit box.
Speaker Pennysays : What do you have a safe deposit box for?
Speaker Sheldonsays : Old comic books. I lost this to Wolowitz in an ill-considered cricket wager.
Speaker Pennysays : What, do they have Wii cricket now? That can’t be very popular.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Penny, I’d rather not talk about it.
Speaker Pennysays : Well, if it makes you feel any better, I’m not feeling so hot either.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Why would that make me feel better?
Speaker Pennysays : I don’t know, empathy? Anyway, I’m just saying that you’re feeling upset about something with Howard, and I’m upset about something with Leonard.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Yes, yes, the disappointing sex. That’s an inexact parallel. You and Leonard can always return to being friends, whereas I can never return to a state in which Wolowitz has not bested me like Mrs. Riley’s chicken.
Speaker Pennysays : What’s Mrs. Riley’s chicken?
Speaker Sheldonsays : A chicken that was owned by Mrs. Riley.
Speaker Pennysays : Okay, forget the chicken.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Well, I wish I could.
Speaker Pennysays : No, no. You may be right about me and Leonard.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Of course I’m right What are the odds I’d be wrong twice in one week?
Speaker Pennysays : No, I mean, we can always go back to being friends.
Speaker Sheldonsays : I just said that. This conversation has started to circle. Meeting adjourned.

Coversation [September 29, 2009, Session #314]:
Speaker Rajsays : Ugh, Toby, what did you do in a past life to be so disgusting now?
Speaker Sheldonsays : His name isn’t Toby. Toby is an absurd name for a cricket.
Speaker Rajsays : What would you name him?
Speaker Sheldonsays : An appropriate cricket name. For example, Jiminy.
Speaker Howardsays : All right, Sheldon, here we go, Kleingast’s Field Guide to North American Insects. Hey, Toby. Right here, see it? The common field cricket, aka Gryllus assimilis, which is Latin for suck it, you lose.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Hang on. Voilà, the snowy tree cricket, aka Oecanthus fultoni, which is Latin for I’ll suck nothing. Of course I’m joking because the Latin for that is nihil exsorbibo.
Speaker Howardsays : That is not Toby, this is Toby. Raj, what do you think?
Speaker Rajsays : Oh, I really don’t care anymore
Speaker Leonardsays : God, I had the most horrible night.
Speaker Rajsays : What happened?
Speaker Sheldonsays : Obviously another carnal fiasco with the shiksi goddess.
Speaker Howardsays : Shiksa. Shik-sa.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Forgive me. Yiddish was not spoken in east Texas. And if it was, it wasn’t spoken for long.
Speaker Howardsays : Yeah, fine, whatever. The point is, you’re wrong again.
Speaker Sheldonsays : We haven’t established that I’m wrong once.
Speaker Howardsays : All right. Tell you what, let’s go down to the Entomology Department and let Professor
Speaker Sheldonsays : He’s a snowy tree cricket, and his name is Jiminy.
Speaker Leonardsays : I bet I could throw a rock in this room and come up with three better friends.

Coversation [November 17, 2011, Session #709]:
Speaker Leonardsays : Hey, can I talk to you about something? It’s a little awkward.
Speaker Sheldonsays : I know what this is about. Given the professional standstill you’re at, you’re wondering if this is the appropriate time to abandon your research and focus on teaching. Yes. And if I may suggest, consider changing disciplines. Yeah, to the humanities, perhaps history. One of the advantages of teaching history is that you don’t have to create things, you know, you just have to remember stuff that happened and then parrot it back. You could have fun with that.
Speaker Leonardsays : Yeah, that’s not it. Stuart’s kind of interested in Amy.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Oh, of course he is. She’s very interesting. Did you know, when she was 14, she severed the webbing between her own toes?
Speaker Leonardsays : No. He wanted me to find out if you’d have a problem with him asking her out.
Speaker Sheldonsays : I’m not sure how to respond, Leonard. I don’t own Amy. Can’t own a person. At least not since? 1863. President Lincoln freed the? Slaves! Come on Leonard, if you’re going to teach history, these are the kind of facts you’ll have to know.
Speaker Leonardsays : You know what? Never mind. I’m going to tell him it’s okay to ask her out.
Speaker Sheldonsays : The question is moot. There is no way that Stuart, an impoverished peddler of picture books, would be at all appealing to Amy Farrah Fowler, a noted neurobiologist capable of performing surgery on her own feet with nothing but nitrous oxide from cans of whipped cream as anesthesia.
Speaker Leonardsays : All right, but just for the record, I checked in with you to see how you’d feel about it.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Fine. The record shall so reflect. Now, getting back to the problem at hand, what to do with a washed-up experimental physicist.
Speaker Leonardsays : I am not washed-up.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Oh, Leonard. Lots of people love you and want to help you, but they can’t until you admit the problem.

Coversation [September 23, 2010, Session #471]:
Speaker Pennysays : Does NASA know you’re using that thing as a napkin holder?
Speaker Howardsays : You kidding? They still think it’s in a secure locker at JPL.
Speaker Pennysays : You stole it?
Speaker Howardsays : Borrowed. The trick is to carry it out to your car like you own it.
Speaker alert)says : Excuse me. Oh. Amy’s at the dry cleaners, and she’s made a very amusing pun. “I don’t care for perchloroethylene, and I don’t like glycol ether.” Get it? She doesn’t like glycol ether. Sounds like either.  L-O-L.
Speaker Pennysays : Who’s Amy?
Speaker Leonardsays : His girlfriend.
Speaker Pennysays : Sheldon has a girlfriend?
Speaker Sheldonsays : She’s not my girlfriend.
Speaker Pennysays : How long has this been going on?
Speaker Leonardsays : Four months.
Speaker Sheldonsays : She’s not my girlfriend.
Speaker Pennysays : Are you telling me, for the past four months I have been asking you what’s new and you never thought to go with Sheldon has a girlfriend?
Speaker Sheldonsays : She’s not my girlfriend.
Speaker Pennysays : Ah, du-du-du-du-du. How did they meet?
Speaker Howardsays : Raj and I entered Sheldon’s information on a dating site, and it spit out Amy Farrah Fowler.
Speaker Pennysays : Oh, my God! Sheldon and Amy.
Speaker Howardsays : Or, as we call them, Shamy.
Speaker (squeals)says : Shamy. I am so digging the Shamy.
Speaker Sheldonsays : All right, everyone pay attention. Yes, I have a friend named Amy Farrah Fowler. Yes, she is female. Yes, we communicate on a daily basis, but no, she is not my girlfriend.
Speaker Pennysays : Okay, well, what do you communicate about?
Speaker Sheldonsays : Well, my work in physics, her work in neurobiology, and most recently, the possibility of our having a child together.
Speaker napkin)says : Thank you.
Speaker Leonardsays : Wait a minute– a child? You never see this girl. You just e-mail and text and Twitter. Now you’re considering having a baby?
Speaker Sheldonsays : Amy pointed out that between the two of us, our genetic material has the potential of producing the first in a line of intellectually superior, benign overlords to guide humanity to a brighter tomorrow.
Speaker Howardsays : I’m guessing that future historians will condemn us for not taking this opportunity to kill Sheldon.
Speaker Pennysays : Okay, I have a question.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Yes, Penny.
Speaker Pennysays : You don’t even like people touching you. How are you going to have sex?
Speaker Sheldonsays : Why on Earth would we have sex?
Speaker Pennysays : Oh, honey, did your mom not have the talk with you? You know, when your private parts started growing?
Speaker Sheldonsays : I’m quite aware of the way humans usually reproduce, which is messy, unsanitary, and based on living next to you for three years, involves loud and unnecessary appeals to a deity.
Speaker Pennysays : Oh, God.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Yes, exactly. Consequently, if Amy and I choose to bring new life into this world, it will be accomplished clinically, with fertility experts in a lab with petri dishes. Which reminds me, you have broad hips and a certain corn-fed vigour. Is your womb available for rental?
Speaker Leonardsays : Still digging the Shamy?
Speaker Pennysays : Look, Sheldon, before you race off to the fertility clinic, you might want to think about, uh gee, I don’t know, maybe actually spending some time with her.
Speaker Sheldonsays : You mean dating?
Speaker Pennysays : Yeah.
Speaker Sheldonsays : I can’t date Amy.
Speaker Pennysays : Why not?
Speaker Sheldonsays : She’s not my girlfriend.
Speaker Pennysays : Okay, look, don’t think of it as dating a girlfriend. Think of it as, uh, getting to know the future mother of your child.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Oh. I hadn’t considered that. I suppose she will have to have access to our progeny. And you don’t think I can achieve the required intimacy via text messaging?
Speaker Pennysays : Probably not.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Huh. It would appear as if the phone companies have been lying to me.

Coversation [September 28, 2009, Session #311]:
Speaker Leonardsays : Sheldon, dinner’s here.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Tandoori Palace?
Speaker Leonardsays : No, we went somewhere new.
Speaker Sheldonsays : You’re good-naturedly ribbing me, aren’t you?
Speaker Leonardsays : No, look, Mumbai Palace.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Why? Why would we change? We had a perfectly good palace. Tandoori Palace is our palace.
Speaker Leonardsays : Trust me, this will be just fine.
Speaker Howardsays : You are the authority on just fine.
Speaker Leonardsays : What’s that supposed to mean?
Speaker him)says : Yeah, exactly. Not bad, but not great.
Speaker Pennysays : What are they talking about?
Speaker Leonardsays : I don’t know
Speaker Sheldonsays : I know. As I’m sure you’re aware…
Speaker Leonardsays : Duh-duh-duh-duh-duh-duh.
Speaker Sheldonsays : If that’s Morse code, that’s terrible. As I was saying, you and Leonard had a disappointing sexual encounter. Earlier this evening, Leonard characterized it as just fine. So what you’re seeing here is a continuation of the mocking that followed.
Speaker Pennysays : Okay, yeah, well, I’m just gonna go eat my dinner elsewhere. Maybe an airplane headed for a mountainside.
Speaker Leonardsays : Penny, wait. Aagh! What is wrong with you?
Speaker Sheldonsays : I sense I may have crossed some sort of line.
Speaker Rajsays : Yeah, you…
Speaker Howardsays : Uh, no, no, no, don’t tell him, let’s see if he can figure it out.
Speaker Pennysays : Ugh, I am so embarrassed!
Speaker Leonardsays : Please don’t be mad.
Speaker Pennysays : What did you tell them?
Speaker Leonardsays : Nothing bad, just that last night was fine.
Speaker Pennysays : Fine? You said it was fine?
Speaker Leonardsays : Yeah, it’s a perfectly good word. You put it in front of wine or dining, and you’ve really got something. Okay, well, let me ask you this, how was last night for you?
Speaker Pennysays : It was… okay.
Speaker Leonardsays : Okay?
Speaker Pennysays : Yeah, it’s a perfectly good word. I mean, you put it in front of dokay and you really got something. All right, look, let’s not overreact, you know? For a lot of couples, it takes time to get to know each other’s rhythms. Learn what the other person wants and likes.
Speaker Leonardsays : So, you’ve been through this before?
Speaker Pennysays : No.
Speaker Leonardsays : Okay-dokay.
Speaker Pennysays : You know, I think this is one of those things where talking about it is not going to make it better. Want a glass of wine?
Speaker Leonardsays : So much.
Speaker Pennysays : Okay. See, we should’ve done this last night, you know, have a little wine, take the edge off.
Speaker Leonardsays : Actually, ethyl alcohol inhibits electrical conduction in the nerve cells and stimulates reuptake receptors of inhibitory neurotransmitters like gamma-aminobutyric acid.
Speaker Pennysays : Don’t talk, just drink.
Speaker Howardsays : No, you’re misunderstanding. A shiksa goddess isn’t an actual goddess and we don’t pray to them, we prey on them.
Speaker Rajsays : Whatever, dude. The point is, Leonard’s got one and you don’t.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Is this it? It was inappropriate to discuss Leonard and Penny’s sex life in front of Leonard and Penny.  Oh, good! Now I can eat.
Speaker Rajsays : What’s that?
Speaker Howardsays : Sounds like a cricket.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Hang on. Based on the number of chirps per minute and the ambient temperature in this room, it is a snowy tree cricket.
Speaker Howardsays : Oh, give me a frickin’ break. How could you possibly know that?
Speaker Sheldonsays : In 1890, Emile Dolbear determined that there was a fixed relationship between the number of chirps per minute of the snowy tree cricket and the ambient temperature. A precise relationship that is not present with ordinary field crickets.
Speaker Rajsays : How do you know what the exact temperature of the room is?
Speaker Sheldonsays : Under the terms of my roommate agreement with Leonard, I’ve had unilateral control of the thermostat ever since the sweaty night of ’06.
Speaker Howardsays : Okay, you were right about Wolverine and bone claws, but you’re wrong about the cricket.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Howard, don’t embarrass yourself, the science chirps for itself. Humorous word play.
Speaker Howardsays : No, no, not this time. I know insects, my friend, I spent many childhood years capturing them with nets, putting them in glass jars, sticking pins through them, mounting them on corrugated cardboard with Dymo labels underneath, identifying the genus and species. In Latin.
Speaker Rajsays : Oh, dude, you are never getting a shiksa goddess.
Speaker Sheldonsays : That is a snowy tree cricket. Oecanthus fultoni. I was done with Latin by fifth grade.
Speaker Howardsays : Okay, okay, tell you what. I am willing to bet anything that’s an ordinary field cricket.
Speaker Sheldonsays : I can’t take your money.
Speaker Howardsays : What’s the matter, you chicken?
Speaker Sheldonsays : I’ve always found that an inappropriate slur. Chickens are not, by nature, at all timid. In fact, when I was young, my neighbour’s chicken got loose and chased me up the big elm tree in front of our house.
Speaker Rajsays : Chickens can’t climb trees
Speaker Sheldonsays : Thank God.
Speaker Howardsays : Okay, I believe a chicken made you his bitch. But the cricket thing, I don’t buy. Bet me.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Fair enough. What stake do you propose?
Speaker Howardsays : I will put up my Fantastic Four number 48, first appearance of Silver Surfer against your Flash 123, the classic Flash of two worlds issue.
Speaker Sheldonsays : All right, you have a wager.
Speaker Howardsays : Hmm.
Speaker Rajsays : Great. Now how are you going to settle it, hmm? There is no way to determine the species of the cricket without examining it.
Speaker Rajsays : I wonder what the non-pathetic people are doing tonight.

[{"role": "user", "content": "Context:
Coversation [September 29, 2009, Session #316]:
Speaker Pennysays : Oh, hey, Sheldon. What you got there? A new comic book?
Speaker Sheldonsays : Old comic book. I just retrieved it from my safe deposit box.
Speaker Pennysays : What do you have a safe deposit box for?
Speaker Sheldonsays : Old comic books. I lost this to Wolowitz in an ill-considered cricket wager.
Speaker Pennysays : What, do they have Wii cricket now? That can’t be very popular.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Penny, I’d rather not talk about it.
Speaker Pennysays : Well, if it makes you feel any better, I’m not feeling so hot either.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Why would that make me feel better?
Speaker Pennysays : I don’t know, empathy? Anyway, I’m just saying that you’re feeling upset about something with Howard, and I’m upset about something with Leonard.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Yes, yes, the disappointing sex. That’s an inexact parallel. You and Leonard can always return to being friends, whereas I can never return to a state in which Wolowitz has not bested me like Mrs. Riley’s chicken.
Speaker Pennysays : What’s Mrs. Riley’s chicken?
Speaker Sheldonsays : A chicken that was owned by Mrs. Riley.
Speaker Pennysays : Okay, forget the chicken.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Well, I wish I could.
Speaker Pennysays : No, no. You may be right about me and Leonard.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Of course I’m right What are the odds I’d be wrong twice in one week?
Speaker Pennysays : No, I mean, we can always go back to being friends.
Speaker Sheldonsays : I just said that. This conversation has started to circle. Meeting adjourned.

Coversation [November 11, 2009, Session #351]:
Speaker Sheldonsays : I should have asked for much more than a comic book and a robot.

Coversation [October 20, 2009, Session #333]:
Speaker Sheldonsays : Got it. Got it. Got it. Got it. Got it. Got it. Betty and Veronica? Ugh.
Speaker Stuartsays : Hey, Sheldon, the new Green Lantern figurine’s coming in tomorrow. Want me to set one aside for you?
Speaker Sheldonsays : Thank you. You just robbed me of the opportunity to stumble upon the figurine and make the oh-so-satisfying journey from discovery to desire to possession.
Speaker Stuartsays : All right, I won’t set one aside for you.
Speaker Sheldonsays : But I must have it.
Speaker Stuartsays : Okay, I’ll set one aside for you.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Thank you. You know, I can buy all these things online. I come here for the personal service.
Speaker Rajsays : Hey, Stuart, is the Wil Wheaton signed up for the mystic warlords tournament THE Wil Wheaton from Star Trek?
Speaker Stuartsays : Yeah, he lives around here. Big gamer.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Excuse me. Are you saying that Wil Wheaton aka Ensign Wesley Crusher on Star Trek:
Speaker Stuartsays : Oh, I’m sorry, did I rob you of the opportunity to stumble onto that for yourself?
Speaker Sheldonsays : You don’t understand. Growing up, I idolized Wil Wheaton? Wesley Crusher had an eidetic memory just like me.
Speaker Rajsays : Oh, what a coincidence. Maybe you can discuss that with him while you’re playing in the tournament. Sign here.
Speaker Sheldonsays : I was such a fan that in 1995, I travelled ten hours by bus to a sci-fi convention in Jackson, Mississippi, wearing my Star Fleet Academy cadet uniform in order to meet Wil Wheaton and get him to autograph my mint in-package Wesley Crusher action figure.
Speaker Rajsays : Ooh, it’ll be like a reunion then. Sign here.
Speaker Sheldonsays : My arduous journey, however, was for naught. Although advertised to appear, he did not show up. It was at that moment, I vowed eternal hatred for Wil Wheaton.
Speaker Rajsays : Okay, great, you can tell him you hate him. Sign here.
Speaker Sheldonsays : It might also interest you to know that Wil Wheaton currently ranks sixth on my all-time enemies list, right between director Joel Schumacher, who nearly destroyed the Batman movie franchise, and Billy Sparks, who lived down the street from me and put dog poop on the handles of my bicycle.
Speaker Rajsays : Okay, I get it, he’s a bad guy. Sign here.
Speaker Sheldonsays : In the words of Khan Noonien Singh in the immortal wrath of Khan, he tasks me, he tasks me and I shall have him.
Speaker Rajsays : No doubt, sign here.
Speaker Sheldonsays : From hell’s heart, I stab at thee.
Speaker Stuartsays : All right, Raj, looks like you’re teamed up with die, Wil Wheaton, die.

Coversation [April 10, 2012, Session #789]:
Speaker Howardsays : Oh, guys, I just remembered. I got you some Hawking souvenirs.
Speaker Leonardsays : What are these?
Speaker Howardsays : Gears and springs from his wheelchair. Pretty cool, huh?
Speaker Rajsays : Wow, that’s amazing.
Speaker Howardsays : Yeah, I made an adjustment on the motor drive and when I was putting it back together I could not for the life of me figure out where they went.
Speaker Sheldonsays : I did it. Had to go to three clothing stores, but we finally found a dress that could envelop your mother.
Speaker Howardsays : I should have sent you to the custom car cover place in Altadena. They have her pattern on file.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Humorous. Now will you please present my paper to Professor Hawking?
Speaker Howardsays : I don’t know.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Oh, for heaven’s sake. I did your laundry, I pee-pee-proofed your belt buckles, I, I even sprained my wrist helping your mother lift her bosom.
Speaker Howardsays : All right, Sheldon, there’s only one thing left I want you to do. Don’t worry, it’s an easy one.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Okay.
Speaker Howardsays : Give me a compliment.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Fine. You have very tiny hands.
Speaker Howardsays : No, about my job. I want you to tell me I’m good at what I do.
Speaker Sheldonsays : You’re obviously good at what you do.
Speaker Howardsays : Well, then why are you always ripping on me?
Speaker Sheldonsays : Oh, I understand the confusion. I have never said that you are not good at what you do. It’s just that what you do is not worth doing.
Speaker Leonardsays : It’s nicer than anything he’s ever said to me. I’d take it and run.
Speaker Howardsays : Thank you, Sheldon.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Now will you give my paper to Hawking?
Speaker Howardsays : Sorry, I can’t.
Speaker Sheldonsays : What, why not?
Speaker Howardsays : I gave it to him three days ago. He was really impressed. He wants to meet you.
Speaker Sheldonsays : All right, then. Thank you, Howard. Please let Professor Hawking know that I’m available at his earliest convenience.
Speaker Rajsays : I thought he might be a little more excited.
Speaker Leonardsays : Give it a second.

Coversation [November 10, 2011, Session #703]:
Speaker Rajsays : Come on, Sheldon, Star Wars.
Speaker Howardsays : I’m pushing play.
Speaker Sheldonsays : A minute.
Speaker Howardsays : If we don’t start soon, George Lucas is going to change it again.
Speaker Sheldonsays : This would go a lot faster if you put your trade school diploma to work and helped me set up this high frequency tone generator.
Speaker Howardsays : I have a Masters degree from M.I.T.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Yeah, but you’ve got a can-do attitude and that’s what’s important.
Speaker Rajsays : I really don’t get your problem with birds.
Speaker Sheldonsays : The question you should be asking is what is their problem with me? My first memory, a hummingbird dive-bombing my stroller to get at the apple juice in my sippy cup.
Speaker Rajsays : Hummingbirds are pretty.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Hummingbirds are the vampires of the flower world.
Speaker Rajsays : Still my first choice for an ankle tattoo. Or a dolphin, I go back and forth.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Age seven, a blood-thirsty chicken chases me up a tree. Age twelve, a magpie tries to steal the retainer out of my mouth. Age sixteen, a parrot in a pet store called me fat ass. Need I go on?
Speaker Rajsays : Yes, please. This is way better than the movie.
Speaker Howardsays : All right, Sheldon, your bird death ray is ready.
Speaker Sheldonsays : It’s not a death ray. It’s just a little ultrasonic blast to scare him off. Trust me, if I had a death ray, I wouldn’t be living here. I’d be in my lair enjoying the money the people of Earth gave me for not using my death ray. All right, and in three, two, one.
Speaker Rajsays : That is one tough birdie.

Coversation [September 29, 2009, Session #314]:
Speaker Rajsays : Ugh, Toby, what did you do in a past life to be so disgusting now?
Speaker Sheldonsays : His name isn’t Toby. Toby is an absurd name for a cricket.
Speaker Rajsays : What would you name him?
Speaker Sheldonsays : An appropriate cricket name. For example, Jiminy.
Speaker Howardsays : All right, Sheldon, here we go, Kleingast’s Field Guide to North American Insects. Hey, Toby. Right here, see it? The common field cricket, aka Gryllus assimilis, which is Latin for suck it, you lose.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Hang on. Voilà, the snowy tree cricket, aka Oecanthus fultoni, which is Latin for I’ll suck nothing. Of course I’m joking because the Latin for that is nihil exsorbibo.
Speaker Howardsays : That is not Toby, this is Toby. Raj, what do you think?
Speaker Rajsays : Oh, I really don’t care anymore
Speaker Leonardsays : God, I had the most horrible night.
Speaker Rajsays : What happened?
Speaker Sheldonsays : Obviously another carnal fiasco with the shiksi goddess.
Speaker Howardsays : Shiksa. Shik-sa.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Forgive me. Yiddish was not spoken in east Texas. And if it was, it wasn’t spoken for long.
Speaker Howardsays : Yeah, fine, whatever. The point is, you’re wrong again.
Speaker Sheldonsays : We haven’t established that I’m wrong once.
Speaker Howardsays : All right. Tell you what, let’s go down to the Entomology Department and let Professor
Speaker Sheldonsays : He’s a snowy tree cricket, and his name is Jiminy.
Speaker Leonardsays : I bet I could throw a rock in this room and come up with three better friends.

Coversation [November 17, 2011, Session #709]:
Speaker Leonardsays : Hey, can I talk to you about something? It’s a little awkward.
Speaker Sheldonsays : I know what this is about. Given the professional standstill you’re at, you’re wondering if this is the appropriate time to abandon your research and focus on teaching. Yes. And if I may suggest, consider changing disciplines. Yeah, to the humanities, perhaps history. One of the advantages of teaching history is that you don’t have to create things, you know, you just have to remember stuff that happened and then parrot it back. You could have fun with that.
Speaker Leonardsays : Yeah, that’s not it. Stuart’s kind of interested in Amy.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Oh, of course he is. She’s very interesting. Did you know, when she was 14, she severed the webbing between her own toes?
Speaker Leonardsays : No. He wanted me to find out if you’d have a problem with him asking her out.
Speaker Sheldonsays : I’m not sure how to respond, Leonard. I don’t own Amy. Can’t own a person. At least not since? 1863. President Lincoln freed the? Slaves! Come on Leonard, if you’re going to teach history, these are the kind of facts you’ll have to know.
Speaker Leonardsays : You know what? Never mind. I’m going to tell him it’s okay to ask her out.
Speaker Sheldonsays : The question is moot. There is no way that Stuart, an impoverished peddler of picture books, would be at all appealing to Amy Farrah Fowler, a noted neurobiologist capable of performing surgery on her own feet with nothing but nitrous oxide from cans of whipped cream as anesthesia.
Speaker Leonardsays : All right, but just for the record, I checked in with you to see how you’d feel about it.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Fine. The record shall so reflect. Now, getting back to the problem at hand, what to do with a washed-up experimental physicist.
Speaker Leonardsays : I am not washed-up.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Oh, Leonard. Lots of people love you and want to help you, but they can’t until you admit the problem.

Coversation [September 23, 2010, Session #471]:
Speaker Pennysays : Does NASA know you’re using that thing as a napkin holder?
Speaker Howardsays : You kidding? They still think it’s in a secure locker at JPL.
Speaker Pennysays : You stole it?
Speaker Howardsays : Borrowed. The trick is to carry it out to your car like you own it.
Speaker alert)says : Excuse me. Oh. Amy’s at the dry cleaners, and she’s made a very amusing pun. “I don’t care for perchloroethylene, and I don’t like glycol ether.” Get it? She doesn’t like glycol ether. Sounds like either.  L-O-L.
Speaker Pennysays : Who’s Amy?
Speaker Leonardsays : His girlfriend.
Speaker Pennysays : Sheldon has a girlfriend?
Speaker Sheldonsays : She’s not my girlfriend.
Speaker Pennysays : How long has this been going on?
Speaker Leonardsays : Four months.
Speaker Sheldonsays : She’s not my girlfriend.
Speaker Pennysays : Are you telling me, for the past four months I have been asking you what’s new and you never thought to go with Sheldon has a girlfriend?
Speaker Sheldonsays : She’s not my girlfriend.
Speaker Pennysays : Ah, du-du-du-du-du. How did they meet?
Speaker Howardsays : Raj and I entered Sheldon’s information on a dating site, and it spit out Amy Farrah Fowler.
Speaker Pennysays : Oh, my God! Sheldon and Amy.
Speaker Howardsays : Or, as we call them, Shamy.
Speaker (squeals)says : Shamy. I am so digging the Shamy.
Speaker Sheldonsays : All right, everyone pay attention. Yes, I have a friend named Amy Farrah Fowler. Yes, she is female. Yes, we communicate on a daily basis, but no, she is not my girlfriend.
Speaker Pennysays : Okay, well, what do you communicate about?
Speaker Sheldonsays : Well, my work in physics, her work in neurobiology, and most recently, the possibility of our having a child together.
Speaker napkin)says : Thank you.
Speaker Leonardsays : Wait a minute– a child? You never see this girl. You just e-mail and text and Twitter. Now you’re considering having a baby?
Speaker Sheldonsays : Amy pointed out that between the two of us, our genetic material has the potential of producing the first in a line of intellectually superior, benign overlords to guide humanity to a brighter tomorrow.
Speaker Howardsays : I’m guessing that future historians will condemn us for not taking this opportunity to kill Sheldon.
Speaker Pennysays : Okay, I have a question.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Yes, Penny.
Speaker Pennysays : You don’t even like people touching you. How are you going to have sex?
Speaker Sheldonsays : Why on Earth would we have sex?
Speaker Pennysays : Oh, honey, did your mom not have the talk with you? You know, when your private parts started growing?
Speaker Sheldonsays : I’m quite aware of the way humans usually reproduce, which is messy, unsanitary, and based on living next to you for three years, involves loud and unnecessary appeals to a deity.
Speaker Pennysays : Oh, God.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Yes, exactly. Consequently, if Amy and I choose to bring new life into this world, it will be accomplished clinically, with fertility experts in a lab with petri dishes. Which reminds me, you have broad hips and a certain corn-fed vigour. Is your womb available for rental?
Speaker Leonardsays : Still digging the Shamy?
Speaker Pennysays : Look, Sheldon, before you race off to the fertility clinic, you might want to think about, uh gee, I don’t know, maybe actually spending some time with her.
Speaker Sheldonsays : You mean dating?
Speaker Pennysays : Yeah.
Speaker Sheldonsays : I can’t date Amy.
Speaker Pennysays : Why not?
Speaker Sheldonsays : She’s not my girlfriend.
Speaker Pennysays : Okay, look, don’t think of it as dating a girlfriend. Think of it as, uh, getting to know the future mother of your child.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Oh. I hadn’t considered that. I suppose she will have to have access to our progeny. And you don’t think I can achieve the required intimacy via text messaging?
Speaker Pennysays : Probably not.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Huh. It would appear as if the phone companies have been lying to me.

Coversation [September 28, 2009, Session #311]:
Speaker Leonardsays : Sheldon, dinner’s here.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Tandoori Palace?
Speaker Leonardsays : No, we went somewhere new.
Speaker Sheldonsays : You’re good-naturedly ribbing me, aren’t you?
Speaker Leonardsays : No, look, Mumbai Palace.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Why? Why would we change? We had a perfectly good palace. Tandoori Palace is our palace.
Speaker Leonardsays : Trust me, this will be just fine.
Speaker Howardsays : You are the authority on just fine.
Speaker Leonardsays : What’s that supposed to mean?
Speaker him)says : Yeah, exactly. Not bad, but not great.
Speaker Pennysays : What are they talking about?
Speaker Leonardsays : I don’t know
Speaker Sheldonsays : I know. As I’m sure you’re aware…
Speaker Leonardsays : Duh-duh-duh-duh-duh-duh.
Speaker Sheldonsays : If that’s Morse code, that’s terrible. As I was saying, you and Leonard had a disappointing sexual encounter. Earlier this evening, Leonard characterized it as just fine. So what you’re seeing here is a continuation of the mocking that followed.
Speaker Pennysays : Okay, yeah, well, I’m just gonna go eat my dinner elsewhere. Maybe an airplane headed for a mountainside.
Speaker Leonardsays : Penny, wait. Aagh! What is wrong with you?
Speaker Sheldonsays : I sense I may have crossed some sort of line.
Speaker Rajsays : Yeah, you…
Speaker Howardsays : Uh, no, no, no, don’t tell him, let’s see if he can figure it out.
Speaker Pennysays : Ugh, I am so embarrassed!
Speaker Leonardsays : Please don’t be mad.
Speaker Pennysays : What did you tell them?
Speaker Leonardsays : Nothing bad, just that last night was fine.
Speaker Pennysays : Fine? You said it was fine?
Speaker Leonardsays : Yeah, it’s a perfectly good word. You put it in front of wine or dining, and you’ve really got something. Okay, well, let me ask you this, how was last night for you?
Speaker Pennysays : It was… okay.
Speaker Leonardsays : Okay?
Speaker Pennysays : Yeah, it’s a perfectly good word. I mean, you put it in front of dokay and you really got something. All right, look, let’s not overreact, you know? For a lot of couples, it takes time to get to know each other’s rhythms. Learn what the other person wants and likes.
Speaker Leonardsays : So, you’ve been through this before?
Speaker Pennysays : No.
Speaker Leonardsays : Okay-dokay.
Speaker Pennysays : You know, I think this is one of those things where talking about it is not going to make it better. Want a glass of wine?
Speaker Leonardsays : So much.
Speaker Pennysays : Okay. See, we should’ve done this last night, you know, have a little wine, take the edge off.
Speaker Leonardsays : Actually, ethyl alcohol inhibits electrical conduction in the nerve cells and stimulates reuptake receptors of inhibitory neurotransmitters like gamma-aminobutyric acid.
Speaker Pennysays : Don’t talk, just drink.
Speaker Howardsays : No, you’re misunderstanding. A shiksa goddess isn’t an actual goddess and we don’t pray to them, we prey on them.
Speaker Rajsays : Whatever, dude. The point is, Leonard’s got one and you don’t.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Is this it? It was inappropriate to discuss Leonard and Penny’s sex life in front of Leonard and Penny.  Oh, good! Now I can eat.
Speaker Rajsays : What’s that?
Speaker Howardsays : Sounds like a cricket.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Hang on. Based on the number of chirps per minute and the ambient temperature in this room, it is a snowy tree cricket.
Speaker Howardsays : Oh, give me a frickin’ break. How could you possibly know that?
Speaker Sheldonsays : In 1890, Emile Dolbear determined that there was a fixed relationship between the number of chirps per minute of the snowy tree cricket and the ambient temperature. A precise relationship that is not present with ordinary field crickets.
Speaker Rajsays : How do you know what the exact temperature of the room is?
Speaker Sheldonsays : Under the terms of my roommate agreement with Leonard, I’ve had unilateral control of the thermostat ever since the sweaty night of ’06.
Speaker Howardsays : Okay, you were right about Wolverine and bone claws, but you’re wrong about the cricket.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Howard, don’t embarrass yourself, the science chirps for itself. Humorous word play.
Speaker Howardsays : No, no, not this time. I know insects, my friend, I spent many childhood years capturing them with nets, putting them in glass jars, sticking pins through them, mounting them on corrugated cardboard with Dymo labels underneath, identifying the genus and species. In Latin.
Speaker Rajsays : Oh, dude, you are never getting a shiksa goddess.
Speaker Sheldonsays : That is a snowy tree cricket. Oecanthus fultoni. I was done with Latin by fifth grade.
Speaker Howardsays : Okay, okay, tell you what. I am willing to bet anything that’s an ordinary field cricket.
Speaker Sheldonsays : I can’t take your money.
Speaker Howardsays : What’s the matter, you chicken?
Speaker Sheldonsays : I’ve always found that an inappropriate slur. Chickens are not, by nature, at all timid. In fact, when I was young, my neighbour’s chicken got loose and chased me up the big elm tree in front of our house.
Speaker Rajsays : Chickens can’t climb trees
Speaker Sheldonsays : Thank God.
Speaker Howardsays : Okay, I believe a chicken made you his bitch. But the cricket thing, I don’t buy. Bet me.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Fair enough. What stake do you propose?
Speaker Howardsays : I will put up my Fantastic Four number 48, first appearance of Silver Surfer against your Flash 123, the classic Flash of two worlds issue.
Speaker Sheldonsays : All right, you have a wager.
Speaker Howardsays : Hmm.
Speaker Rajsays : Great. Now how are you going to settle it, hmm? There is no way to determine the species of the cricket without examining it.
Speaker Rajsays : I wonder what the non-pathetic people are doing tonight.
